critique.summary: The article announces the release of FFmpeg 8.1 "Hoare" and summarizes recent major releases and features of the FFmpeg multimedia framework. It highlights new codec support, Vulkan compute-based codecs, hardware acceleration improvements, and internal optimizations across multiple versions.
mainPoints: FFmpeg 8.1 "Hoare" introduces new decoders including experimental xHE-AAC Mps212 and MPEG-H decoding, EXIF metadata parsing, and Vulkan compute-based codec support for ProRes and DPX.
FFmpeg 8.0 "Huffman" added native decoders like APV and ProRes RAW, Vulkan compute-based codecs for FFv1 and ProRes RAW, and hardware accelerated encoding and decoding for various codecs.
FFmpeg 7.1 "Péter" stabilized the VVC decoder, added native AAC USAC decoder, MV-HEVC decoding, and Vulkan encoding support for H264 and HEVC.
The project has modernized infrastructure, improved internal APIs, and enhanced hardware acceleration support across releases.
FFmpeg is an open-source, widely used multimedia framework for audio and video processing, with continuous improvements and community contributions.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are consistent with the official FFmpeg release notes and announcements available on the FFmpeg website and related technical blogs. The described features such as Vulkan compute-based codecs, new decoders, hardware acceleration improvements, and infrastructure upgrades align with documented FFmpeg development progress. The article accurately reflects the incremental nature of FFmpeg releases and the community-driven development model. No unsupported or false claims were identified.
considerations: The article does not mention potential compatibility issues or hardware requirements for Vulkan-based codecs and hardware acceleration features.
There is no discussion of licensing considerations or patent issues related to some codecs, which can be relevant for commercial use.
Performance impacts and stability of experimental features like the xHE-AAC Mps212 decoder are not detailed.
The article does not provide user guidance on migration or upgrading from previous FFmpeg versions.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the new features and updates in FFmpeg 8.1, with users sharing insights, opinions, and questions about the release. Some comments express appreciation for FFmpeg while others critique its complexity.

comments.author: gyan
comments.text:
Changelog:

ffprobe -codec option

EXIF Metadata Parsing

gfxcapture: Windows.Graphics.Capture based window/monitor capture

hxvs demuxer for HXVS/HXVT IP camera format

MPEG-H 3D Audio decoding via mpeghdec

D3D12 H.264 encoder

drawvg filter via libcairo

ffmpeg CLI tiled HEIF support

D3D12 AV1 encoder

ProRes Vulkan hwaccel

DPX Vulkan hwaccel

Rockchip H.264/HEVC hardware encoder

Add vf_scale_d3d12 filter

JPEG-XS parser

JPEG-XS decoder and encoder through libsvtjpegxs

JPEG-XS raw bitstream muxer and demuxer

IAMF Projection mode Ambisonic Audio Elements muxing and demuxing

Add vf_mestimate_d3d12 filter

xHE-AAC Mps212 decoding support (experimental)

Remove the old HLS protocol handler

Vulkan compute codec optimizations

swscale Vulkan support

LCEVC metadata bitstream filter

Add vf_deinterlace_d3d12 filter

ffprobe: only show refs field in stream section when reading frames

ProRes Vulkan encoder

LCEVC parser

LCEVC enhancement layer exporting in MPEG-TS
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and informative overview of the new features in FFmpeg 8.1, showcasing a good understanding of the topic.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text:
FFmpeg is really great. The only wish I'd have is for the usage to become simpler - both for regular stuff, but also for advanced filtering.

If anyone remembers, avisynth was pretty cool back in the days. You could kind of script video/audio manipulations, a bit like a UNIX/Linux pipe, but kind of simpler, in my opinion. FFmpeg allows for many similar operations, but remembering anything here is ... hard. I'd love for the whole usage API to become much simpler, but it seems nobody among the ffmpeg dev team is considering this. :(

I can't be the only one with that wish though ...

It does not diminish ffmpeg being so great in general, but I think it could be better.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a valid concern about the usability of FFmpeg while acknowledging its strengths, making it a constructive contribution.

comments.author: pandaforce
comments.text: Khronos published a post on the Vulkan compute codecs in FFmpeg: https://www.khronos.org/blog/video-encoding-and-decoding-wit...
comments.analysis: This comment shares relevant information about Vulkan compute codecs but lacks depth in analysis or personal insight.

comments.author: brcmthrowaway
comments.text: How much of this release was done by to corporate/big tech employees?
comments.analysis: This comment raises a potentially contentious question without providing context or constructive feedback, making it less valuable.

comments.author: megous
comments.text: I wouldn't get too excited about rockchip hw encoding. It's rkmpp based, not an upstream solution. You'd need Rockchip kernel for this, I guess.
comments.analysis: While this comment offers a cautionary perspective on Rockchip hardware encoding, it could benefit from more elaboration on its implications.

comments.author: junelee77
comments.analysis: This comment is overly simplistic and does not add any substantial value to the discussion.
